other_observers: There were two other observers present, one a photograher, with a gigantic lens.; Boulder Bird Club with William Kaempfer as the leader, found this bird on 06/02/2007.
specific_location: One half mile south on CR 45 from its intersection with CR 122, just west of the depression called Coal Creek.
description: In a small evaporative pool the two species that stood out were American Avocets, and a tame, bright rufous/chestnut ventrally, phalarope with a stout yellow black-tipped bill. This bird had a blackish crown and nape. The gleaming white checks were set off by the contrasting colors of the bird's dark eye, dark area behind the bill, the blackish crown and the rich chestnut chin throat and neck.   The blackish scapulars and tertials were broadly edged with gold and white, adding even more color to this bird. There were about four noticeable still whitish breast feathers, and under the wings quite a few broadly tipped gray feathers remained from basic plumage (see photo). At times I could see the upper tibia and the leg color appeared dull greenish.   The bill was blunt tipped.Behaviors: I watched this bird for an hour from the side of the rapidly drying small pond on Pawnee National Grasslands. The bird (similar to this species in Alaska) showed no fear of human intrusion. The bird feed 95% of the time, flew off (not people related) about 250 feet from the east side of the pond, and returned to feed, all within approxiametely five seconds, as if testing its musculature.  This bird was an active feeder, constantly moving about mostly the shallowest parts of the pond, moving around clockwise and covering the entire edge of the pond. It ignored three birders standing at different locations on the shoreline and even came directly at me to withing 30 feet, to pull a tadpole shrimp out of the shallows. I watched it also feed by swiping small insects out of the air, those being directly in the path of the birds paddling.   The bird did preen for about five minutes. It stood near a small rock but remained in water up to its belly (see photos).Call: none heardPlumage: mostly molted to alternate
photographer: Photographer: bill maynard, bill maynard, bill maynard
